coredata.dc:description: In recent years, multi-dimensional sensing methods based on multi-emission MOFs have attracted great attention from researchers due to their excellent sensitivity and selectivity to the target analytes. Through the mixed ligand strategy or encapsulating the appropriate fluorophores (lanthanide ions, luminescent ligands, metal ligand complexes, organic dyes, carbon dots, etc.) in the MOFs channel, MOF-based proportional sensors with multiple luminescent centers can be successfully designed. In this paper, the research progress of multi-dimensional sensors based on MOFs in recent years is reviewed, with emphasis on the preparation of three-emission MOF composites and their applications in detecting ions, pH, volatile organic compounds and organic molecules. This paper will be of great significance for researchers interested in multi-dimensional sensing based on three-emission MOFs to understand its research progress.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of multi-emission MOF composites for multi-dimensional sensing application

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The construction of multi-emission center MOFs and its high-dimensional sensing detection of analytes have broad development prospects. This paper focuses on the preparation strategies of MOFs with three or more luminescent centers in recent years, and then discusses the recognition ability of high-dimensional sensing based on multi-luminescent MOFs to target analytes.

In recent years, multi-dimensional sensing methods based on multi-emission MOFs have attracted great attention from researchers due to their excellent sensitivity and selectivity to the target analytes. Through the mixed ligand strategy or encapsulating the appropriate fluorophores (lanthanide ions, luminescent ligands, metal ligand complexes, organic dyes, carbon dots, etc.) in the MOFs channel, MOF-based proportional sensors with multiple luminescent centers can be successfully designed. In this paper, the research progress of multi-dimensional sensors based on MOFs in recent years is reviewed, with emphasis on the preparation of three-emission MOF composites and their applications in detecting ions, pH, volatile organic compounds and organic molecules. This paper will be of great significance for researchers interested in multi-dimensional sensing based on three-emission MOFs to understand its research progress.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The construction of novel Metal-Organic Frameworks (MOFs) has been a hot research topic in recent years. MOFs are usually organic–inorganic hybrid materials with infinitely extended uniform structures constructed by organic ligands and metal nodes.[1,2] MOFs have ultra-high porosity and specific surface area compared with traditional porous materials (activated carbon, zeolite, etc.),[3–5] which gives them important application prospects in gas adsorption and separation storage,[6–8] catalytic conversion,[9–12] magnetism,[13–15] electronic devices,[16–18] and identification sensing,[19–23] etc.

MOF materials exhibit excellent fluorescence properties due to the unique synergistic interaction between metal and conjugated ligands. In addition, the aperture size, ligand structure and central coordination ion also affect the fluorescence emission effect of MOFs. According to the unique assembly mode of MOF materials, the main sources of fluorescence are metal center luminescence, ligand-center luminescence, metal–ligand charge transfer (MLCT) or ligand–metal charge transfer (LMCT), functionalized induced luminescence and introduced guest molecule luminescence.[24–28] At present, researchers prefer to construct functional MOFs with two or more luminous centers using multiple ligands or introducing different guest molecules. MOFs have many fascinating advantages over traditional fluorescence detection materials. 1). Due to the tunability of their structures, MOFs constructed with appropriate molecular design concepts can detect various types of analytes, such as anions and cations, organic molecules, biotoxins, etc. 2). Compared with traditional detection instruments such as chromatography and ultra-high liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry, MOF-based sensors are simple and easy to prepare, low labor costs, and easy to achieve real-time detection. 3). The excellent recyclability makes the MOFs-based sensors economically accepted.[29–31].

Conventional sensing methods that rely on “turn-on” or “turn-off” responses between the sensor and analyte lack robustness and can suffer from deviations in detection results due to variations in instrument parameters and environmental factors. As a result, there is growing interest in the development of highly selective and stable luminescence sensors that offer superior performance under diverse experimental conditions.[32–34] In order to avoid this phenomenon, additional fluorescence signals are introduced into these sensors, so that they have effective internal reference capabilities and the color change can be detected by the visual inspection system, thereby improving the detection accuracy of the analyte.[35] Since the luminescence color of the dual-emission sensor is between the two primary colors, the human visual monitoring system is not sensitive to the color change in this range. In contrast, for three-emission sensor materials the obvious color difference before and after detection and the color change in a wider range are more easily perceived by the naked eye. The sensor arrays built based on the wavelength ratio of multiple emission centers or by adding parameters such as time and concentration are more innovative. The design concept of multi-dimensional detection sensor is embodied in obtaining information from multiple transmission channels to achieve accurate monitoring and identification of target analytes. High-dimensional sensing detection technology can not only accurately detect multiple analytes at the same time, but also distinguish complex mixtures with similar chemical structure and performance, which is more suitable for the analysis and testing of real samples.[29,30,36] Therefore, in order to improve the detection accuracy of analytes, the development of MOF-based multi-dimensional ratio sensors is extremely urgent.

At present, some articles have reported the preparation strategies and applications of multi-luminescent MOF materials. Yin et al. reported the research results of their group and others, but the paper is mainly about the summary of MOF materials with dual emission centers, and only some reports are about MOF materials with three or more emission centers.[37,38] This paper mainly introduces the preparation and application of three-emission MOF materials. In addition, multi-emission MOF materials are widely used, but there is a lack of targeted summary in the field of fluorescence sensing. Only a few articles have specifically reported the research of triluminescent MOF materials in the field of fluorescence sensing. Therefore, this paper will be of great significance for researchers interested in multi-dimensional sensing based on three-emission MOFs to understand its research progress.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis strategy of multi-emission MOF composites
ce:section.ce:para: This section mainly introduces several reasonably designed multi-emission MOF composite material strategies. The multi-ligand mixed strategy and the use of MOFs as a substrate to embed guest molecules have been proved to be feasible. The fluorophore used to construct multi-emission MOF composites should have high quantum yield and absorption coefficient under the excitation of light source, so that it is bright enough for visual observation. In addition, the strong binding ability of MOF substrates to fluorophores is also one of the reference standards. At present, the fluorophores that meet the above conditions include lanthanide ions, luminescent ligands, metal ligand complexes, organic dyes, carbon dots, etc. The advantages and disadvantages of several fluorescent guest molecules are summarized in Table 1 .

The mixed ligand strategy provides a simple and feasible solution for the construction of multi-luminescent MOF materials. Multiple emission phenomena occur after a single excitation of multiple ligands, and the target can be efficiently perceived based on the combination of emission differences. Yin et al.'s group provided several methods for constructing multi-emission MOF materials using mixed ligands. The 5-boronoisophthalic acid (5-bop) and isophthalic acid (1, 3-BDC) were mixed in a molar ratio of 1: 1, and Eu3+ and Tb3+ were used as metal nodes to obtain Eu-MOF with red emission and Tb-MOF with green emission under 275 nm excitation. Under the same excitation, Uio-66-NH2 can achieve strong blue emission. Based on the excellent emission colors of the three substances, according to the RGB theory, the panchromatic fluorescence image was successfully obtained under the excitation of 275 nm.[39] This scheme attempts to use terephthalic acid (BDC), 2-aminoterephthalic acid (BDC-NH2), and 2,5-dihydroxylterephthalic acid [BDC-(OH)2] to construct a mixed ligand MOF. It was found that the emission intensity of BDC was very weak, incompatible with BDC-NH2 and easily crosstalk with the emission of BDC-(OH)2. Therefore, they chose BDC-NH2, BDC-(OH)2 as mixed ligands and Zn2+ to construct MOF. The luminescent behavior of the MOF material was studied when the ratio of the two ligands was 1: 4, 1: 9, 1: 19, 1: 24. Under 310 nm excitation, MOF exhibits strong emission at 450 nm and 550 nm. As the ratio decreases, the emission at 450 nm gradually decreases, while the emission at 550 nm gradually increases. Importantly, white light emission was finally achieved when the ligand ratio was 1: 19 and the excitation wavelength was 330 nm.[40] Another example is the successful preparation of Eu-DATA/BDC using tterephthalic acid (H2BDC), 2,5-diaminoterephthalic acid (H2DATA) and Eu3+. When excited at 270 nm, the material has multiple characteristic emission peaks from the ligand and Eu3+.[41] In addition, multi-emission MOF materials can be constructed by first synthesizing functionalized ligands and then combining metal ions. The flexible ligand L1 [1,3,5-tris(5-methoxy-1,3-benzene dicarboxylic acid)benzene] was constructed by connecting three isophthalic acids to a benzene ring using a flexible-CH2-O-, or tetraphenylpyrazine was modified with four carboxyl groups to form 2,3,5,6-tetrakis(4-carboxyphenyl)pyrazine. These two functionalized ligands can interact with Eu3+ to obtain MOFs with multi-emission properties.[42,43] It has been proved to be a feasible scheme to design multi-emission MOF materials by mixing multiple ligands, but most of the multi-emission MOF materials prepared by this method in the existing examples are dual-emission. Therefore, the design and synthesis of MOF materials with more emission centers by using ligand luminescence should be an important research direction in the future.

Lanthanide MOFs possess multiply characteristic fluorescence peculiarity. For instance, high fluorescence quantum yield, long fluorescence emission lifetime, large Stokes displacements, sharp linear bands, have gradually become a research hotspot in the field of developing multi-luminescent MOFs.[44–46] Lanthanide metal ions with high coordination number and high charge can easily combine with oxygen-supplied ligands or nitrogen-supplied ligands. Except for La3+ and Lu3+, the incompletely filled 4f electronic layer structure of Ln3+ can produce f-f or f-d leap emission, and the synergistic effect of ions and ligands can be used to synthesize Ln-MOF materials with diverse structures and good luminescence performance.[47] Among the lanthanide ion, Tb3+ and Eu3+ are good luminescent centers that emit characteristic red and green light when irradiated by UV light, which are often used to form luminescent Ln-MOFs.

Gao et al. prepared Eu0.67Tb0.33-TCPB from the solvent heat reaction of LnCl3-6H2O with rigid ligand 1,3,5-tris(1-(2-carboxyphenyl)-1H-pyrazol-3-yl) benzene (H3TCPB), which is a highly selective luminescent sensor with three emission centers derived from the ligand, Eu3+ and Tb3+. It was used to detect a variety of nitroaromatic hydrocarbons and achieved a three-dimensional proportional sensing of CrO4 2−/Cr2O7 2−.[53] X et al. Prepared Eu3+/Tb3+ co-doped bismuth-based MOF (Eu0.005Tb0.095-Bi0.9-MOF) by an in situ synthesis method under solvothermal conditions. BiOBr and H3btc were used to synthesize the blue-emitting Bi-MOF, which is the blue emission of the ligand at 420 nm. The Eu3+/Tb3+@Bi-MOF composite obtained after doping has additional green emission (543 nm) and red emission (614 nm). By adjusting the doping ratio of ligands and two lanthanide ions, white light emission originating from blue-green–red can be achieved (Fig. 3 ).[54] Li et al. prepared {[Zn3(L)2·2(Me2NH)]·3(CH3OH)·5(H2O)}n(HPU-14) using ZnCl2 and H4L. Subsequently, the HPU-14 powder was soaked in the nitrate solution of Tb3+ and Eu3+. Due to the ion exchange process, Tb3+ and Eu3+ replaced (CH3)NH2 + in the HPU-14 channel. When the concentration ratio of Tb3+ and Eu3+ was 5:4, the composite material HPU-14@Tb3+@Eu3+ with optimal white light emission was successfully constructed. As a good encapsulation host, HPU-14 can not only protect the luminescence of lanthanide ions, but also provide corresponding binding sites to interact with lanthanide ions.[55] However, the high cost and rare variety of lanthanides limit the development of multi-emission MOFs centered on lanthanide ions.

[Ru(by3)]2+, [IrCF3-ppy-F2)2(bpy)]+ and [pt(ppy)(NCMe)2]+ are metal–ligand complexes that are capable of stable luminescence in aqueous or organic solutions. Due to its excellent chemical stability and characteristic luminescence stability, it has gradually become an ideal luminescence center for the synthesis of tricolor MOF composites. A novel dual-ratio luminescent probe D [(0.017 wt％[Pt]++0.046 wt％[Ru]2+)@1] was synthesized by encapsulating [Ru(by3)]2+ (red-emitting) and [pt(ppy)(NCMe)2]+ (green-emitting) with blue-emitting Zn-MOF as the matrix. Different from the traditional qualitative detection and three-dimensional coding recognition of NAC vapor based on time as a three-dimensional variable, D realizes the quantitative detection and three-dimensional coding identification of liquidoid NAC and metal ions by adding titration as the third dimension.[56] Zhao et al. reported another encapsulation example, introducing [Ru(by3)]2+ and [Ir(CF3-ppy-F2)2(bpy)]+ (green-emitting)into Zn-MOF by ion exchange method. Both composites can achieve white light emission by adjusting the molar ratio and emission intensity.[57] Moreover, Ni et al. built a tricolor luminescent MOF composite Z3 (0.91 wt%[Pt]+ + 1.10 wt%[Ru1]2+)@bio-MOF-1)，which is formed by encapsulating [Pt(MeCN)2bpy]+ (green-emitting) and [Ru(bpy)2(MebpyCHO)]2+([Ru1]2+) (red-emitting) into bio-MOF-1 with blue emission. It is worth noting that the aldehyde group introduced here can enhance the luminescence intensity of the light-emitting module, thereby affecting the color change before and after the sensor operation (Fig. 4 ).[58] However, the high price of precious metals leads to the lack of metal–ligand complexes. Therefore, there are not many examples of multi-emission MOFs based on metal–ligand complexes.

A white-emitting MOF (TCPP0.29 ⊂ UiO-66-(NH2)0.62(diOH)0.38) was synthesized by combining three luminescent ligands into the UiO-66 framework. BDC-NH2 produced a blue fluorescence emission peak at 428 nm and BDC-diOH produced a green fluorescence emission peak at 540 nm, and the size of BDC-diOH (2,5-dihydroxy terephthalic acid) is close to terephthalic acid, the original ligand of UiO-66, so it could replace the terephthalic acid ligand in the synthesis of UiO-66. TCPP has a long emission wavelength at 645 nm and is integrated into UiO-66 as the red emission part (Fig. 5 ).[59] White light emission was obtained by adjusting the ratio of three ligands for proportional fluorescence sensing of alkaloids.

Organic dye molecules have the advantages of wide emission width, rapid radiation emission, and high quantum yield.[60,61] Combining organic dyes with MOF materials can effectively increase the luminescence rate and expand the emission range of MOF. Zhao et al. successfully integrated fluorescein isothiocyanate, platinum meso-tetra (4-carboxyphenyl) porphyrin and the reference dye 1,3,6,8-tetra (4-carboxyphenyl) pyrene on the UiO-66 nanoparticle substrate at the same time, and the sensor had a better performance when the doping ratio of the three dyes was 1:25:6, which realized the simultaneous detection of different signals (Fig. 6 ).[62] Ni et al. constructed a metal–organic framework [Pb3(L)3(H2O)2]·10DMF·6.5H2O (1) by mixing DMF (dimethylformamide), Pb2+ and H2L (4-(5,7-dioxopyrrolo[3,4-f] benzimidazole-6-y) l, and loaded [Ru(bpy)3]2+, [Ru]2+ and fluorescein into 1 to constitute complex G1. The optimal ratio of the composite covering the entire visible light spectrum is 1.261 wt% FRS + 0.463 wt% [Ru]2+@1, and the three main emission peaks are located at 457 nm, 534 nm and 588 nm, respectively.[63] He et al. selected Rhodamine and fluorescein of appropriate size, low cost and strong fluorescence emission as packaging guests to load into IRMOF-3, and constructed three-color white light composite RhB/FL@IRMOF-3. Compared with the fluorescence emission of IRMOF-3 alone, the composite produces red and green emission at 540 nm and 590 nm when excited at 365 nm.[64] The technology of encapsulating dyes is still immature. On the one hand, special attention should be paid to avoid the emission peaks of the MOF material being blocked by the emission peaks of the encapsulated dyes, which requires strict and precise control of the dye ratio. On the other hand, it is necessary to avoid the problem that the luminescence quenching or dye falling caused by the aggregation of dyes encapsulated in MOF affects the stability of materials.[65,66].

Carbon dots are new type of electroluminescent, photoluminescent, and chemiluminescent fluorescent nanomaterials with many advantages over other carbon nanomaterials or traditional dyes, such as non-toxicity, good water solubility, good absorption of visible light and the ability to act as a good electron acceptor or electron donor.[67–70] Based on the above advantages, a growing number of researchers are now showing a preference for carbon points. In recent years, the incorporation of carbon dots into MOFs has also been a research approach to develop a multi-emission composite. Gao et al. synthesized a CDs@EuW-MOF complex that can detect water in water-methanol mixtures as well as acetonitrile in solvent mixtures. First, citric acid, urea, and distilled water were heated in a microwave oven for five minutes, and then the CDs used for the experiments were obtained by drying and centrifuging. Then CDs were added to the solution of Eu(NO3)3·5H2O, 5-methyl-2-pyrazine carboxylic acid (Hmpca), and K4[W(CN)8]-4H2O mixed and reacted for ten minutes to obtain CDs@EuW-MOF. The CDs@EuW-MOF have multi-emission behavior under single excitation, and the emission spectra in moethanol indicate five characteristic emission peaks of Eu3+ at 582 nm, 596 nm, 617 nm, 655 nm, and 705 nm, as well as two-color emission of CDs in green at 520 nm and blue at 450 nm (Fig. 7 ).[71] Nevertheless, the low fluorescence yield of carbon dots and their size mismatch with the pore size of MOFs are also common problems.

The combination strategy of multi-ligand mixing and introducing guest molecules has also been proved to be feasible. The MOF substrate was prepared by using 2,5-dihydroxyterephthalic acid (H2BDC-diOH), 5,8-dicarboxynaphthalene-2-sulfonic acid sodium (H2NDC-SO3Na) as mixed ligands and Zr4+. Methylene blue (MB) was introduced into MOF by immersion method, The blue-emitting anionic ligand H2NDC-SO3Na stabilizes the MB guest by electrostatic attraction. The composite material has three emission bands at 432 nm, 535 nm and 677 nm, respectively.[72].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multi-dimensional sensing based on multi-emission MOF sensors
ce:section.ce:para: The energy transfer after the interaction of multiple luminescent centers with the analyte can induce the sensor to produce a change in the intensity ratio of multiple emission peaks, thus forming a set of two-dimensional or three-dimensional fingerprints. This multi-dimensional sensing not only has internal self-calibration function, but also can solve the influence of non-analyte concentration factors such as excitation light intensity and sensing material concentration. In this section, the chemical sensing behavior of MOF composites with multiple luminescent centers as sensors was introduced, and the multi-dimensional identification and detection of ions, pH, VOCs, and organic molecules were summarized in Table 2 .

Hexavalent chromium Cr (VI) (CrO4 2-, Cr2O7 2-) is widely used in various types of industrial production, but it is highly toxic and can accumulate in humans and other organisms through water or air, which can lead to many kinds of cancer and cause a severe threat to human health and the environment.[73–75] Therefore, the accurate and rapid detection of toxic anionic contaminants such as CrO4 2- and Cr2O7 2- is of fundamentality to human health and environmental protection. Gao et al. prepared a three-emission organic framework material Eu0.67Tb0.33-TCPB, which showed excellent detection results for 4-nitrophenol, CrO4 2- and Cr2O7 2-. The unique combination of two ratios of ligand peaks and lanthanide ion characteristic emission peaks (I386/I545, I386/I616) with CrO4 2-/Cr2O7 2- concentrations allows for 3D ratio sensing. The detection limits of chromate are as low as 0.32 × 10−6/0.28 × 10−6 M, and the detection limit of Cr2O7 2- is as low as 0.34 × 10−6/0.30 × 10−6 M (Fig. 8 ).[53].

Fe3+, Cu2+, Co2+ and other metal cations are important metal cations that are ubiquitous in biochemical processes and industrial production, but their harm to organisms and the environment cannot be underestimated.[76,77] Therefore, in addition to toxic anions, the detection of certain metal cations has also received extensive attention from researchers. Guan et al. introduced Eu3+ and organic dye 3,3′-diethyloxacarbocyanine iodide (DOC) into MOF-253, and successfully constructed MOF-253 with three luminescent centers. The three characteristic emissions of ligand, DOC and Eu3+ are at 420 nm, 520 nm and 614 nm. Ag+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Co2+ and Ni2+ can lead to different degrees of quenching of these three sensor units. Based on the mutual effect between these five ions and the N-position of pyridine, the absorption wavelength of Fe3+ overlaps with the excitation spectra of MOF, showing that there is a competitive absorption between them. The energy transfer process exists between Ni2+, Co2+ and ligands and DOC. Therefore, the system can accurately detect Ag+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Co2+ and Ni2+, and distinguish binary or ternary mixtures of Fe3+, Co2+ and Ni2+. In addition, Ag+ and Cu2+ can be quantitatively detected in the scope of 0–15 μM, and the detection limits are 5.45 μM and 0.81 μM, respectively.[77] The white light composite D ((0.017 wt％[Pt]+ + 0.046 wt％[Ru]2+)@1) has triple emission characteristics. The 2D fingerprint constructed by the ratio of the emission peak height of the ligand to the ELMS can distinguish Fe3+, Al3+, Cu2+ and Co2+. After adding the concentration as the third dimension, Fe3+ and Al3+ have their own characteristic distribution in the three-dimensional fingerprint. More importantly, the mixture of Al3+ cause a decrease in the energy transfer efficiency of the ligand to [Ru]2+. Therefore, with the addition of Al3+, the luminescence of D changes from white to blue. In addition, it can be clearly identified by the naked eye that Fe3+ completely quenches the luminescence of D, which is achieved by the fluorescence resonance energy transfer effect of the ligand to Fe3+ and the collapse of the MOF skeleton caused by Fe3+ (Fig. 9 ).[56].

pH plays a crucial role in understanding human physiology or pathological processes, and the many common diseases can be diagnosed with the aid of a pH, such as certain common gynecological diseases, kidney stones, Alzheimer's disease, etc.[78,79] The sensitive fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) (Igreen, 480–600 nm), the oxygen-sensitive phosphorescent dye multi-dentate ligand of platinum meso-tetra(4-carboxyphenyl) porphyrin (Pt-TCPP) (Ired, 600–720 nm) and the luminescent ligand 1,3,6,8-tetra(4-carboxylphenyl) pyrene (TBAPY) (Iblue, 400–480 nm) were encapsulated in UiO-66 and successfully constructed as a three-channel proportional probe that can simultaneously detect changes in oxygen and pH in living cancer cells. The blue light from TBAPY, which is basically constant, was chosen as the internal reference unit, and Igreen/Iblue was gradually enhanced with the increase of pH. The signal change was obvious in the range of 5–8, and the fluorescence response was most sensitive at 6.5. Meanwhile, the red luminescence from Pt-TCPP gradually decreased with increasing oxygen concentration (0–30 mg/L). In addition, the calculation results showed that the probe had a good detection ability for pH and oxygen, and the detection limits were 0.0459 mg/L and 0.1314 mg/L.[63].

VOCs in the environment is a new type of gaseous pollutants that have an impact on environmental quality and it is also a serious risk to human health. Long-term exposure to toxic VOCs can lead to a variety of acute and chronic health problems, such as neurological damage, respiratory inflammation, deformities, etc.[80–82] Zhao investigated the ability of W2 (a triple-emission white light composite), which for multi-dimensional ratio sensing of VOCs. In the orthogonal combinations of IMOF/I[Ir] + and IMOF/I[Ru] 2+, each data point corresponds to a VOC. In addition, it is possible to quantitatively detect VOCs and clearly distinguish aromatic molecules of similar structures such as xylene, benzene, and toluene (Fig. 10 ).[57] The three emission MOF sensor {MOF(1 = [Pb3(L)3(H2O)2]·10DMF·6.5H2O)}was prepared by encapsulating FRS and complex cation [Ru(by3)]2+. The sensor shows triple-emission behavior from the frame and two chromophores. Decoding graphs of IMOF/IFRS and IMOF/I[Ru] 2+ combinations distinguish most VOCs other than Ben, F-Ben, and Cl-Ben. In the DMF environment, the numerical value of IMOF/I[Ru] 2+ increased from 0.91 to 1.93, after irradiation by a 365 nm UV lamp, the color of the MOF changed from white to brown, indicating its superiority in detecting DMF (Fig. 11 ).[64] Sun et al. synthesized Eu0.059Tb0.051Gd0.89-DPON, which is a new type of white light emitting composite. Due to the fluorescence quenching caused by the interplay between the carbonyl group in formaldehyde and the lanthanide ions, the fluorescent material was proved to be a fluorescent probe for detecting formaldehyde. In addition, the three-dimensional decoding diagram based on the ratio of three emission centers of the fluorescent material (I544/I491, I616/I592 and I415/I544) achieves effective detection of different solvent molecules. CH3COOH, p-Xylene, Cyclohexane, Toluene, CH2Cl2, CHCl3, DMA, DMF, CH3CN, EtOH, DMSO and benzene have their own unique positions in the 3D diagram, even CH2Cl2 and CHCl3 with similar structure can be clearly distinguished.[49].

In modern agricultural production, the output of high-quality and high-yield agricultural products is inseparable from the use of pesticides. While benefiting, the harm caused by pesticides cannot be underestimated. Pesticides can be well dissolved in water, not easily degraded, and easily accumulated in ecosystems. On the one hand, long-term exposure to pesticides can cause many physical diseases, such as liver failure, neurological dysfunction, respiratory diseases, etc. On the other hand, it will pose a serious threat to the environment, such as polluting air and water and killing beneficial organisms.[89,90] Tb-BDOA was synthesized from Tb (III) and H2BDOA. Tb-BDOA has multiple emission characteristics of ligand at 445 nm and Tb (III) at 488 nm and 545 nm. According to the changes of I445/I488 and I445/I545 at different concentrations, 3D coding recognition was carried out. Since the energy level of NSF (41493 cm−1) is higher than that of the ligand (37052 cm−1), it is beneficial to the energy transfer from NSF to the ligand, thereby enhancing the fluorescence intensity of the ligand. The fluorescence quenching of the Tb (III) characteristic peak is due to the energy transfer process from NSF to the ligand, which hinders the ''antenna effect''. There is energy competitive absorption between thiamethoxam (TMT) and Tb-BDOA. Therefore, Tb-BDOA has excellent detection ability for nicosulfuron (NSF) and thiamethoxam (TMT), and the detection limits are 1.61 μM and 1.04 μM, respectively (Fig. 17 ).[91].

This paper reviews the research progress of MOF sensors with multiple luminescent centers. Due to the versatility of ligands and metal ions, it is possible to adjust the luminescent properties of MOFs by embedding fluorophore molecules (lanthanide ions, luminescent ligands, metal ligand complexes, organic dyes and carbon dots) or changing ligands to construct a multi-ratio sensor. Obviously, the target analyte detection based on multi-ratio sensing has more advantages than the traditional single-peak measurement, because of its self-calibration ability and naked eye recognition function, and also allows multiple analyte detection. In this review, we discuss the synthesis methods of multi-emission MOFs and the multi-dimensional sensing detection of ions, pH, volatile organic compounds and organic molecules.

However, there are still some practical problems in these guest molecules used to construct multi-luminescent MOF materials: 1) The types and reserves of lanthanide elements are limited and expensive, which limits the development and application of multi-luminescent MOFs based on lanthanide elements. 2) Lack of diversity is a significant shortcoming of luminescent metal–ligand complexes. 3) Fluorescent dyes are cheap and easy to obtain luminescence, but the problem that the fluorescence intensity is significantly reduced due to dye agglomeration needs to be solved. 4) At present, the deficiency of carbon dots is the low fluorescence yield. Whether the size of the prepared carbon dots and the pore size of the MOFs are suitable is also an important prerequisite for the successful construction of multi-luminescent MOF composites. Farther, we believe that the future work needs to focus on the stability of MOFs. The main component of the environment and biological media is water, and the stability of most MOFs in water needs to be improved. The pH of the solution also affects the structure of MOFs, so it is necessary to develop stable MOF materials. In addition, regarding the interaction between the MOF host and the target analyte, the core principles involved in the current sensing mechanism are still quite vague and limited to qualitative analysis. Therefore, it is necessary to use theoretical calculations for more in-depth quantitative research. Most of the multi-luminescence center ratio sensors are in the experimental stage, and the development of practical application sensors is still a challenging task. It is necessary to further study the practical application of sensors to achieve lower detection limits for target analytes and the requirements of not being affected by interfering substances to cope with the challenges faced by complex systems.
